content.text:
Yes. In the researcher’s publication records (2010–2026), many papers include as coauthors people who she supervised (e.g., students/postdocs such as **Daniel Sundfeld**, **Edans Flávio de Oliveira Sandes**, **Marco Antônio Figueiredo**, **Gustavo Jardim Portella**, **Carlos Antônio Campos Jorge**, etc., as shown in the supervision list).

Examples from the publications list that match supervised advisees:
- **Sundfeld, Daniel** (coauthor) — e.g., *Using GPU to accelerate…* (2020) and *…pairwise structural RNA alignment* (2016/2020 items).
- **Sandes, Edans Flávio de Oliveira** (coauthor) — multiple items, e.g., *MASA* (2016), and several later GPU/alignment papers.
- **Figueiredo Jr, Marco Antonio Caldas de** (coauthor) — e.g., *Parallel… with pruning* / related items in 2019–2022.
- **Portella, Gustavo Jardim** (coauthor) — e.g., *A Novel Statistical and Neural Network Combined Approach…* (2023).

**Limitation:** the provided data doesn’t include an explicit “advisee vs. coauthor” flag, so I can’t compute a precise percentage of her publications that include advisees—only that coauthorship with supervised researchers appears to be a recurring pattern.
